footer.brandDescription: Exploring the scientific resonance of Yin-Yang philosophy across quantum physics, systems science, information theory and other frontiers. A dialogue bridging ancient wisdom and modern inquiry.
footer.quote: One yin and one yang — this is called the Dao. What inherits it is goodness; what completes it is nature.

intro.col1Body: The Taiji diagram (太极图, "yīn-yáng symbol") is one of the world's oldest visual metaphors. A circle split by an S-shaped curve into black and white halves — each containing a dot of the opposite color — captures the idea that opposites are not enemies but partners in a dynamic, ever-turning dance.
intro.col2Body: Yin (black) represents receptivity, stillness, and the hidden; Yang (white) represents activity, expression, and the visible. But neither can exist without the other — night defines day, rest enables action, and every peak contains the seed of its own reversal. This is not mysticism: it is a model of polarity and mutual dependence.
intro.col3Body: Here, we test a bold hypothesis: that these ancient patterns — duality, complementarity, emergence from opposition — show up across modern science in surprising ways. From quantum particles to neural networks, the Taiji diagram offers a unifying visual language for understanding how complexity arises from simple, balanced principles.

quiz.cumulativeDescription: One question from each domain — test your understanding of Taiji principles across all scientific fields.

tour.stepMath: Taiji Math & Bagua — The binary foundations of yin-yang mapping to 3-bit arithmetic, discovered by Leibniz in 1701.
tour.stepQuantum: Quantum Entanglement — Paired particles mirroring yin-yang mutual dependence across any distance.
tour.stepSymmetry: Symmetry Breaking — How the universe's fundamental forces emerged from an undifferentiated state, like yin-yang from Wuji.
tour.stepInfo: Information Theory — Entropy as a measure of uncertainty, eliminating chaos to reveal structure — pure yang emerging from yin.
tour.stepChaos: Chaos & Fractals — Order hiding within apparent randomness, infinite self-similarity — the Taiji within the Taiji.
tour.stepSystems: Systems Science — Emergence: how simple yin-yang rules at the micro level self-organize into macroscopic complexity.
